coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots decorated magnetic ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles with enhanced adsorption capacity for the removal of dye from aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Weilong
Guo, Feng
Wang, Huibo
Liu, Changan
Fu, Yijun
Yuan, Songliu
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Widely used synthetic dyes have been caused serious environmental pollution. Therefore, it is imperative to acquire highly efficient adsorbent to remove them. Here, we report the carbon dots/ZnFe2O4 (CDs/ZFO) composites were prepared through a facile hydrothermal route for absorption removal of dye from aqueous solution. The characterizations reveal the CDs were uniformly deposited on the surfaces of ZFO nanoparticles in the composite. The CDs/ZFO composites as adsorbents exhibit enhanced adsorption behavior for methyl orange (MO) in comparison of pristine ZFO, in which the 5% CDs/ZFO (with the CDs mass content of 5wt%) shows the highest absorption activity. Experimental studies on adsorption isotherms of MO over the 5% CDs/ZFO composite indicate that experimental data were found to follow Langmuir model with a monolayer adsorption capacity of 181.2mgg−1. The corresponding adsorption kinetics was fitted well with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. Moreover, thermodynamics parameters including ΔG°, ΔH° and ΔS° were tested, demonstrating that the adsorption of MO over CDs/ZFO composite was spontaneous and exothermic in nature. The remarkably increased adsorption performance of CDs/ZFO composites can be attributed to abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
ZnFe2O4
Magnetic composite
Methyl orange
Adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ZnFe2O4 and CDs/ZnFe2O4 nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, (c) FT-IR spectra and (d) Nitrogen adsorption isotherms and measured parameters of ZFO and 5% CDs/ZFO. (Inset of Fig. 1d is the photograph of magnetic separation of CDs/ZFO composite from the aqueous solution with a permanent magnet.).
TEM images of (a) ZFO and (b) 5% CDs/ZFO. (c) HRTEM and (d) corresponding mapping images of 5% CDs/ZFO.
High resolution XPS spectra of (a) Zn 2p, (b) Fe 2p, (c) O 1s, and (d) C 1s in 5% CDs/ZFO composite.

(a) The adsorption of MO (20mgL−1) over as-prepared samples (inset shows change in color of MO in the presence of 5% CDs/ZFO in aqueous solution). Adsorption kinetics of dyes over the 5% CDs/ZFO: (b) pseudo-first-order, (c) pseudo-second-order and (d) Elovich models.

The effect of (a) ionic strength, (b) initial pH of dye solution and (c) temperature on adsorption of MO (20mgL−1) over 5% CDs/ZFO. (d) Van’t Hoff plot for the adsorption of the MO onto 5% CDs/ZFO composite.
(a) Langmuir and (b) Freundlich adsorption isotherm of MO onto 5% CDs/ZFO at 20°C.
Comparison of the pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, and Elovich models for adsorption of MO on 5% CDs/ZFO composite.
Thermodynamic parameters for the adsorption of MO on 5% CDs/ZFO.
Langmuir and Freundlich parameters for the adsorption of MO on 5% CDs/ZFO at 20°C.
Comparison of MO adsorption capacities of various adsorbents.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots decorated magnetic ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles with enhanced adsorption capacity for the removal of dye from aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices, Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices
Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Subsurface Hydrology and Ecological Effects in Arid Region, Ministry of Education, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Chang’an University, Xi’an 710064, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Subsurface Hydrology and Ecological Effects in Arid Region
School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite (CDs/ZFO) was synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. Methyl orange (MO) was analyzed as the adsorption model to investigate the adsorption performance of as-prepared products. The CDs/ZFO composite adsorbents exhibited superior adsorption behavior in comparison of pristine ZFO, and 5% CDs/ZFO (with the CDs mass content of 5wt%) showed the highest absorption activity. Experimental studies on adsorption isotherms and kinetics of MO over the CDs/ZFO composite indicated that experimental data was found to follow Langmuir model with a monolayer adsorption capacity of 181.2mgg−1, and adsorption kinetics fitted well with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. Moreover, thermodynamics parameters including ΔG°, ΔH° and ΔS° were tested, demonstrating that the adsorption of MO over CDs/ZFO composite was spontaneous and exothermic in nature.

Widely used synthetic dyes have been caused serious environmental pollution. Therefore, it is imperative to acquire highly efficient adsorbent to remove them. Here, we report the carbon dots/ZnFe2O4 (CDs/ZFO) composites were prepared through a facile hydrothermal route for absorption removal of dye from aqueous solution. The characterizations reveal the CDs were uniformly deposited on the surfaces of ZFO nanoparticles in the composite. The CDs/ZFO composites as adsorbents exhibit enhanced adsorption behavior for methyl orange (MO) in comparison of pristine ZFO, in which the 5% CDs/ZFO (with the CDs mass content of 5wt%) shows the highest absorption activity. Experimental studies on adsorption isotherms of MO over the 5% CDs/ZFO composite indicate that experimental data were found to follow Langmuir model with a monolayer adsorption capacity of 181.2mgg−1. The corresponding adsorption kinetics was fitted well with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. Moreover, thermodynamics parameters including ΔG°, ΔH° and ΔS° were tested, demonstrating that the adsorption of MO over CDs/ZFO composite was spontaneous and exothermic in nature. The remarkably increased adsorption performance of CDs/ZFO composites can be attributed to abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic dyes are widely used in the manufacture of textile, paper, plastic, leather, and are the most common organic pollutants in industrial waste water, which pose a serious threat to the ecosystem due to their high toxicity and possible accumulation in the environment [1,2]. Hence, the removal of dyes from wastewaters is still highly desirable. Some treatment techniques, such as chemical oxidation [3], membrane filtration [4] and photocatalytic degradation [5] and adsorption [6,7], have been developed for removing dyes from aqueous solutions. Among these techniques, adsorption has been found to be an efficient and economic process for the efficient removal of various organic contaminants from water. Owing to its high surface area and porous structure, activated carbon is often used as an adsorbent to remove certain classes of chemical pollutants in treating wastewater [8,9]. However, filtration, a method of conventional separation of activated carbon, may cause blockage of filters or the loss of carbon, which is also a time-consuming process. Therefore, activated carbon is generally discarded after sewage treatment, which can easily lead to the secondary pollution [10,11]. Crucially, it is essential to pursue more ‘ideal’ alternatives to activated carbon possessing high adsorption capacity, relatively inexpensive production and easy recyclability.

In recent years, magnetic separation technology has received extensive attention in solving environmental problems. Ferrites of the type MFe2O4 (M is a divalent metal cation) are magnetic materials with the cubic spinel structure, which have been widely used in treatment of water [12–14]. Among ferrites, ZnFe2O4 is an interesting magnetic material with moderate saturation magnetization, excellent chemical stability and mechanical hardness, which exhibited the excellent adsorptive properties with the highly effective recovery of the magnetic separation technique [14–17]. Nevertheless, its adsorption capacity is still far from enough, and further enhancement of its adsorption properties is required.

Carbon dots (CDs) with abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface, as the latest member in nano carbon family, have found wide applications such as fluorescent probes, photovoltaic devices, bioimaging, thanks to its excellent optical properties, low toxicity, chemical stability and water dispersity [18–20]. Previous reports have demonstrated that the functional groups (OH, COOH, etc.) are helpful for improving the adsorption performance of reactants [21,22]. Therefore, we attempted to combine CDs with magnetic ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles to construct a high adsorption capacity composite for efficient removal organic pollutants from aqueous solution.

In this work, a simple one-pot hydrothermal method was employed to develop a new kind of magnetic adsorbent, CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite (CDs/ZFO). Methyl orange (MO) was analyzed as the adsorption model to investigate the adsorption performance of as-prepared products. The CDs/ZFO composites adsorbents exhibit superior adsorption behavior in comparison of pristine ZFO, in which the 5% CDs/ZFO (with the CDs mass content of 5wt%) shows the highest absorption activity. Experimental studies on adsorption isotherms and kinetics of MO over the CDs/ZFO composite indicated that experimental data were found to follow Langmuir model with a monolayer adsorption capacity of 181.2mgg−1, and adsorption kinetics fitted well with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. Moreover, thermodynamics parameters including ΔG°, ΔH° and ΔS° were tested, demonstrating that the adsorption of MO over CDs/ZFO composite was spontaneous and exothermic in nature.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and without purification. Graphite rods (99.99%) were purchased from Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd., USA. Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, NaOH and methyl orange (MO) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. The chemical structure of MO is shown in Fig. 1 .

The CDs powders were prepared by an electrochemical method [23]. Generally, the two graphite rods were inserted into ultrapure water in parallel using as an anode and cathode, and a static potential of 30V was applied to the two electrodes for 120h. Then, the CDs solution was formed. After filtration for three times, the prepared CDs solution was treated in a freeze-drying way to obtain the CDs solid powders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnFe2O4 and CDs/ZnFe2O4 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 35mL of Zn(NO3)2 solution (0.2M), 35mL of Fe(NO3)3 solution (0.4M) and 1.0g CTAB were mixed and stirred for 30min. Then, the homogeneous solution was adjusted to pH 10 using NaOH solution (0.1M) and transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a capacity of 100mL. Afterwards, the autoclave was heated at 130°C for 24h in an electric oven. When the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature, the brick red suspension of ZnFe2O4 was collected and washed with ethanol and deionized water for several times to remove impurities, and finally dried at 75°C for 6h in a vacuum oven. For preparation of CDs/ZnFe2O4 nanocomposites, the CDs solid powders with different weight ratios (1wt.%, 3wt.%, 5wt.% and 10wt.%) were added to the fabricated process of ZnFe2O4, which were abbreviated as 1% CDs/ZFO, 3% CDs/ZFO, 5% CDs/ZFO and 10% CDs/ZFO samples.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded using an X‘Pert-ProMPD (Holand) D/max-γA X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.154178nm). Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was characterized by a Nicolet 360 spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded on an HR 800 Raman spectroscope (JY, France). Transmission electron microscope (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) were performed with a FEI-Tecnai F20 microscope operating at 200kV, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromatised Al Kα X-ray source. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas and pore structures were characterized by a Micromeritics ASAP-2050 porosimeter. The equilibrium concentrations of dyes were determined at 460nm for MO using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750).

Adsorption experiments were carried out in break (250mL), where solution of MO (200mL) with dye concentration of 20mgL−1 and 0.03g of adsorbent was placed. The break with solution was placed in a constant temperature bath and agitated by magnetic stirrer. To investigate the influence of temperature, five different temperatures with 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60°C were set in adsorption experiments.
Prior to mixing with the adsorbent, a few drops of 0.1M HCl or 0.1M NaOH were added for adjusting the pH of the dye solution.

When the adsorption equilibrium period reached, 3mL aqueous sample was extracted from the solution and separated from the adsorbent magnetically. And concentration of MO was analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy at a wavelength of 460nm. The amount of MO adsorbed at equilibrium qe (mgg−1) was calculated by following equation:
(1)
q e
=
(
C 0
−
C e
)
V
m
where C0 (mgL−1) is the initial dye concentration, Ce (mgL−1) the dye concentration at equilibrium, V (L) the volume of the solution, and m (g) is the mass of the adsorbent.

The procedures of kinetic experiments were identical with those of equilibrium tests. At given time intervals, 3mL aqueous samples extracted from the solution and separated from the adsorbent magnetically. And concentrations of MO were analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy at a wavelength of 460nm. The amount of MO adsorbed at time t qt (mgg−1) was calculated by following equation:
(2)
q t
=
(
C 0
−
C t
)
V
m
where Ct (mgL−1) the dye concentration at any time t.

The crystalline structure and phase composition of the prepared samples were analyzed by XRD. Fig. 2 a shows the XRD patterns of the ZFO and 5% CDs/ZFO. The XRD pattern for ZFO can be perfectly indexed to the cubic spinel structure (JPCDS 1-1109), and no characteristic peaks of impurities was detected. The peaks at 2θ=30.1, 35.2, 42.8, 52.9, 56.5 and 67.8° can be assigned to the reflection of (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes of ZnFe2O4, respectively. In the case of 5% CDs/ZFO, the main characteristic diffraction peaks are similar to those of ZFO. It is noted that the XRD peak of CDs at nearly 26° is not observed, which could be ascribed to the small amount and relatively low diffraction intensity of CDs [24]. The Raman spectra of the ZFO and 5% CDs/ZFO are shown in Fig. 2b. Compared with ZFO, two prominent peaks centered at around 1325 and 1584cm−1 in the 5% CDs/ZFO, which are ascribed to the D band of the presence of sp3 defects and G band due to in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon [25,26], demonstrating that CDs/ZFO composite was constructed. The FT-IR spectra of ZFO and 5% CDs/ZFO are displayed in Fig. 2c. The FT-IR spectrum of ZFO reveals that three characteristic peaks at about 550, 415 and 3443cm−1 are correspond to the stretching vibrations of the Zn-O bonds in tetrahedral positions, the Fe-O bonds in octahedral positions and adsorbed H2O, respectively [27]. For 5% CDs/ZFO, besides the characteristic peaks of ZFO, the stretching vibrations of CO and CC are observed at 1395 and 1636cm−1 in the composite [28], ascribing to some new functional groups and the oxygen-containing groups of the CDs, which contributes to enhancing the adsorption of dye molecules. Meanwhile, the pore structures of ZFO and 5% CDs/ZFO were studied to investigate Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area by N2 adsorption/desorption analysis (Fig. 2d). The SBET of 5% CDs/ZFO (116.8m2/g) is a little higher than that of ZFO (103.9m2/g), and the pore volumes of samples are 0.19 and 0.27cm3 g−1, respectively, revealing that the introduction of CDs favors the enhancement of adsorption capacity of ZFO due to more surface sites for the adsorption of dye molecules. In addition, considering the good magnetic performance of ZFO, we collected CDs/ZFO composite from the aqueous solution with a permanent magnet. From the photographs (inset in Fig. 2d), the as-prepared CDs/ZFO composites can be attracted by the magnet in a short period, demonstrating high magnetic sensitivity. Consequently, the CDs/ZFO composites adsorbents can be rapidly separated under an applied magnetic field.

The morphology and crystalline structure of as-prepared materials were depicted by TEM images shown in Fig. 3 . The TEM in Fig. 3a displays the spherical shape of the ZFO nanoparticles with diameter below 10nm, which is in agreement with the broad XRD diffraction peak. After coupling the CDs, ZFO remains the original shape and size distribution (Fig. 3b). As shown in Fig. 3c, the lattice fringe spacing of 0.25nm in the 5% CDs/ZFO ascribes to the (311) plane of the cubic-structured ZFO [29]. The other lattice spacing of nearly 0.21nm along the side of the ZFO corresponds to the (100) planes of graphitic carbon [30]. In addition, the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) mappings of C, Zn, Fe, and O further confirm the co-existence of CDs and ZFO (Fig. 3d).

XPS spectra were applied to elucidate the specific bonding and chemical states of the elements in 5% CDs/ZFO composite. The full XPS survey is presented in Fig. S1, demonstrating that C, Zn, Fe and O are the main elements in the sample. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the high-resolution Zn 2p spectrum displays two characteristic peaks centered at 1022.4 and 1045.6eV, which ascribe to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively [31]. In the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 4b), it shows two typical peaks located at 711.2 and 752.1eV with another two relatively obvious satellite peaks at 719.6 and 733.2eV, which correspond to the Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively [32]. Meanwhile, the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 529.5, 531.1 and 532.1eV, respectively, among of which the former two peaks (529.eV and 531.1eV) arises from the lattice oxygen and the chemisorbed and dissociated oxygen species, respectively [33,34], and the last peak (532.1eV) is related to the CO bond [35]. In Fig. 4d, the C 1s high-resolution spectrum can be fitted into three peaks located at 284.6eV, 285.6eV and 288.1eV, corresponding to CC, CO and CO, respectively [35].

The adsorption of MO (20mgL−1) was conducted under dark at room temperature to evaluate the adsorption capacities of as-prepared products (Fig. 5 a). The results reveal that the adsorption of MO over all the as-synthesized samples increased with time and then attained equilibrium after 40min. The adsorption processes are of all samples initially rapid, and then slow, perhaps because a large number of vacant surface sites were available for adsorption during the initial stage, and then, the remaining vacant surface sites were difficult to occupy because of the repulsive forces between the dye molecules on adsorbents [36]. Notably, the adsorption capacity of ZFO at equilibrium increases from 75.5 to 109.7mgg−1 with increasing the contents of CDs from 1 to 5wt.%, demonstrating that the introduction of CDs into ZFO could provide more vacant surface sites for improving the adsorption activity. Further increasing the content of CDs over 5wt.% leads an obvious decrease of adsorption capacity. This reduction of adsorption activity may be ascribed to that redundant CDs could cover or occupy adsorption active sites and channels on the surface of ZFO. For further confirming this point, we also measured the N2 adsorption isotherms of 1% CDs/ZFO, 3% CDs/ZFO and 10% CDs/ZFO (Fig. S2). We found that 5% CDs/ZFO adsorbent possesses the largest surface area and pore volume compared with others’. Generally, the adsorption performance of adsorbents is strongly associated with the surface areas. The larger surface area is, the more vacant surface sites provide, which is benefit to improving the adsorption activity. Thus, 5% CDs/ZFO composite exhibits the highest adsorption performance for removal MO from aqueous solutions. To further investigate the kinetic adsorption data, Lagergren pseudo-first-order kinetic model, the pseudo-second-order model and Elovich models were employed. These are pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order and Elovich kinetic models as represented in Eqs. (3)–(5), respectively [37]:
(3)
ln
(
q e
−
q t
)
=
lnq e
−
k 1
t
2.303
(4)
t
q t
=
1
k 2
q e 2
−
t
q e
(5)
q t
= b + alnt
Where k1 (min−1) and k2 (gmg−1 min−1) are the adsorption rate constant of pseudo-first and second-order adsorptions, respectively; a and b are Elovich constants.

The kinetic data from different kinetic models are depicted in Fig. 5b and c. All the kinetic parameters determined are summarized in Table 1 . The linear plot of t/qt versus t (pseudo-second-order kinetic model) shows greater fitness of the straight line (R2 >0.99), and the calculated qe value (qe, cal) agrees very well with the experimental data value (qe, exp) compare with other kinetic models. Consequently, these results imply that the applicability of the pseudo-second-order kinetic model to analyze the adsorption process of MO onto 5% CDs/ZFO composite is feasible.

The effect of ionic strength on adsorption of MO (20mgL−1) over 5% CDs/ZFO was explored in NaCl solutions (0–1.6mol/L) at the temperature of 20°C and shown in Fig. 6 a. The result reveals that with an increase in the NaCl concentration from 0 to 1.6mol/L, the adsorption equilibrium of MO decreased from 109.7 to 45.4mgg−1. This is because when the electrostatic attraction between the adsorbent surface and adsorbate ions, increasing ionic strength will reduce the adsorption capacity. As presented in Fig. 6b, we also studied the influence of initial pH value on adsorption capacity of MO (20mgL−1) onto 5% CDs/ZFO at the temperature of 20°C. It can be seen that there is no significant influence of pH on the adsorption of MO onto the composite although maximum adsorption capacity of MO was 113.9mgg−1 observed at pH 5. In addition, the detailed effect of temperature on adsorption of MO over 5% CDs/ZFO was investigated and illustrated in Fig. 6c. It was found that with the increase in temperature from 20 to 60°C, the adsorption equilibrium of MO decreased from 109.7 to 75.5mgg−1. There are two reasons which may leads to the decrease of the adsorption capacity: (i) due to the fact that the adsorption of dyes in this system is an exothermic process, the increase of temperature retards the positive reaction [14,38]; (ii) the surface tension of adsorbent decreases at higher temperatures, resulting in the decreasing penetration of reactive dyes inside micropores [39]. For further exploring the thermodynamics of adsorption of MO on 5% CDs/ZFO, three basic thermodynamic parameters, enthalpy (ΔH°), entropy (ΔS°) and Gibbs free energy (ΔG°), were calculated in following equations [40]:
(6)
lnK a
=
Δ
S °
R
−
Δ
H °
RT
(7)
K a
=
q e
C e
(8)
Δ
G ° = − RT
lnK a
Where R is the gas constant, T is the solution temperature (K) and Ka is the adsorption equilibrium constant. Enthalpy (ΔH°) and entropy (ΔS°) were calculated from the slope and intercept from the plot of lnqe/Ce versus 1/T (Fig. 6d), and the value of Gibbs free energy (ΔG°) was calculated using Eq. (8). The above calculated results are presented in Table 2 . The negative value of ΔH° (−25.86kJmol−1) discloses that adsorption of MO onto 5% CDs/ZFO is an exothermic process. The values of Gibbs free energy (ΔG°) were negative in the temperature range of 20–60°C, confirming that the adsorption of MO onto 5% CDs/ZFO was spontaneous and thermodynamically favorable. When the temperature increased from 20 to 60°C, ΔG° increased from −8.29 to −5.98kJmol−1, suggesting that adsorption was spontaneous at low temperatures. The negative value ΔS° (−59.2Jmol−1 K−1) indicates a decrease in the degree of freedom of adsorption MO onto 5% CDs/ZFO. Molecules before adsorption can move in three dimensions but as they get adsorbed on the surface, the motion of molecules is restricted toward the surface and their disorderness decreased resulting in the decrease in entropy [41].

The equilibrium adsorption isotherm is fundamental in investigating the interactive behavior between solutes and adsorbent in adsorption system. Fig. S3 shows the effect of initial dye concentration on adsorption of MO onto 5% CDs/ZFO, indicating that the adsorption capacities of MO increase with increasing of the dye concentration. In order to further investigate the equilibrium of adsorption, the well-known Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were employed. Langmuir isotherm theory is based on the assumption of adsorption on a homogeneous surface. The Langmuir’s isotherm model is represented in the following equation [42]:
(9)
C e
q e
=
1
Q 0
b
+
C e
Q 0
Where Q0 (mgg−1) is the maximum mass adsorbed under saturation conditions per mass unit of adsorbent, and b (Lmg−1) is the Langmuir isotherm constant. The values of Q0 and b were calculated from the slope and intercept of the linear plot Ce/qe versus Ce (Fig. 7 a). The maximum monolayer adsorption capacity Q0 was 181.2mg MO per gram of the 5% CDs/ZFO. The fundamental characteristics of the Langmuir isotherm can be expressed in terms of a dimensionless equilibrium parameter (RL), which is defined as the following equation [43]:
(10)
R L
=
1
1 +
bC 0
Where C0 (mgL−1) is the highest initial concentration of the adsorbate. The RL value indicates the type of the isotherm to be either unfavorable (RL
> 1), linear (RL =1), favorable (0<RL <1), or irreversible (RL =0). The values of RL were found to be 0.69 and confirmed that the 5% CDs/ZFO is favorable for adsorption of MO under the conditions used in this work.

The Freundlich isotherm describes equilibrium on heterogeneous surfaces and is not restricted to the formation of monolayer capacity. The Freundlich model is given by the following equation [44]:
(11)
lnq e
=
lnK F
+
(
1 n
)
lnC e
Where KF (mgg−1 (L/mg)1/n) and n are Freundlich constants, which represent adsorption capacity and adsorption strength, respectively. The values of KF and n were calculated from the slope and intercept of the linear plot lnqe versus lnCe (Fig. 7b). The values of n ranging from 1 to 10 indicated a good adsorption process.

Table 3 showed the values of Langmuir and Freundlich constants, and the correlation coefficients R2 obtained from the linear regression. The results reveal that the fit of the experimental data for the adsorption of MO onto 5% CDs/ZFO suggests that Langmuir model (R2 =0.991) gave closer fittings than the Freundlich model (R2 =0.979).

The removal of MO by different adsorbents has been studied extensively. Table 4 compares the monolayer adsorption capacities of the CDs/ZFO composite with different adsorbents previously used for removal of MO dyes. It can be seen from Table 4 that the adsorption capacities of CDs/ZFO for MO dye are much higher than that of many other previously reported adsorbents, indicating that the as-prepared CDs/ZFO magnetic composite can be considered a promising adsorbent for MO dye removal from aqueous solutions.

We have prepared CDs/ZFO composites through a facile hydrothermal route and used as adsorbent for the removal of MO from aqueous solution. The CDs/ZFO composites as adsorbents exhibit superior adsorption behavior in comparison of pristine ZFO, in which 5% CDs/ZFO (with the CDs mass content of 5wt%) shows the highest absorption activity. Experimental studies on adsorption isotherms and kinetics of MO over the 5% CDs/ZFO composite indicated that experimental data was found to follow Langmuir model with a monolayer adsorption capacity of 181.2mgg−1, and adsorption kinetics fitted well with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. Moreover, thermodynamics parameters including ΔG°, ΔH° and ΔS° were tested, demonstrating that the adsorption of MO over CDs/ZFO composite was spontaneous and exothermic in nature. It was indicated that the as-prepared composite could be used as a promising and effective adsorbent for the removal of organic pollutants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21771132, 51422207, 51572179, 21471106, 21501126), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20161216) and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.10.099.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
